title: Negation in Senhaja zrqet
introduction: Across languages, saying “no” or denying something is done in a few common ways. According to the provided framework, the three main patterns are: specialized negative particles (about half of languages), negative affixes (about a third), and negative verbs (about one in eight). English mostly uses a negative particle like “not” (This is not a dog), and sometimes uses negative-like prefixes such as “im-” in impossible. English also has words like “never,” “no,” “nothing,” and so on. In Senhaja zrqet, the core pattern is a two-part negation around the verb, with a marker before the verb and another after it. There are also special forms for negating nouns and for saying that something does not exist. You will see items like **u/ur/uḏ** before a verb and **-š/-ši** after it, the particle **maši ḏ** before nouns for “not a … / not the …,” and existential negation like **makayen** “there isn’t.”

sections.focus: Most frequent pattern: verbal bipartite negation (u … -š)

example.description: Simple sentence negation of a process with the discontinuous verbal negator **u … -š** around the verb.
Negation of a mental state using **u … -š** on the finite verb.

example.source_sentence: We did not begin in the morning.
example.description: Past/process negation with **u … -š** attached to the verb form; note hyphenation/spelling variation is common.

example.source_sentence: he arose that man, he does not see well.
example.description: The preverbal **uḏ** and postverbal **-š** frame the verb to deny the ongoing state.
Perfect/state negation with the same **u … -š** frame.

example.source_sentence: No, no! We won’t be bathing.
example.description: Future/process negation; the postverbal element **-š** is visible; spacing can vary.
Negative imperative built with the same **u … -š** frame on the 2nd-person form.
Another negative imperative with **u … -š**.
Negation of ability with the same **u … -š** pattern fused to the verb.
Documented example of the same bipartite pattern **uḏ … š** for verbal negation.
Verbal sentence negation with **uḏ … š**.
Perfective negation with **uḏ … š** around the verb.

sections.description: Both sources agree that the most frequent way to negate clauses with verbs is a discontinuous negation wrapped around the finite verb: a preverbal element like **u/ur/uḏ** plus a postverbal element like **-š/-ši**. The sentence analysis calls this a verbal circumfix (sometimes written attached to the verb), while the documents call them negative particles placed before and after the verb. Orthography varies: you will see spaced forms (e.g., **u tussuġš**) and fused forms (e.g., **uttqeddayaġš**); the postverbal piece can appear as **-š** or **-ši**. This same frame is used across process/state predicates, mental predicates, ability, and negative imperatives.

sections.focus: Nominal/equative negation and identification: maši ḏ and negated ‘be’

example.target_sentence: walakin maši ḏ arḇa ɛaḏ, mmġuraġ daḇa.
example.source_sentence: But I’m not a child any more, I’ve grown up.
example.description: Nominal/equative negation with **maši ḏ + NP**; also shows the aspectual adverb **ɛaḏ** ‘any more/no longer’.

example.target_sentence: waš maši ḏ ddefṭar n ttamarin nnem?
example.description: Negative question on identification using **maši ḏ** before the noun phrase.

example.target_sentence: Maši ḏa ẓẓuwar, ġir ḏ aḵeššuḏ.
example.source_sentence: It's not a root, it's just a branch.
example.description: Category exclusion with **maši ḏ** negating inclusion in a category (‘not a root’).
Documents show a negated ‘be’ construction with **u … š** around the copular/locative element to deny an attribute.
Documents show **maši** used as a standalone negator with non-verbal predicates for identification/association.
Postverbal **-ši** appears with **ma-** before the predicate, showing another nominal/adjectival negation pattern in the data.
Documents give a Taghzut example with a non-verbal predicate negated without the bipartite verbal frame.
Documents illustrate successive negation elements (**u … šay**) with an adjectival predicate (Taghzut).

sections.description: To say “X is not Y” or “This is not a Y,” Senhaja zrqet often uses **maši ḏ + NP** before the noun phrase. The documents also show negation of non-verbal predicates with a negated ‘be’ structure (**u … š**) and with **ma- … -ši** around the predicate. These forms cover both ‘not in a category’ and ‘not identical to a specific entity’ (identification).

sections.focus: Existential and localization negation: makayen, walu, and ‘remain’

example.description: Existential negation with **makayen** ‘there is not,’ strengthened by **ḥetta ši** ‘any at all’ before the noun.
Localization/existential denial with **walu** ‘nothing’, i.e., ‘there is nothing here.’

example.target_sentence: U lmeddraṣa qḏima dba gaɛ u teqqim.
example.source_sentence: That was an old school that doesn’t exist anymore.
example.description: Non-existence expressed by negating a ‘remain/stay’ verb (**u teqqim**) with an ‘anymore’ context.

example.target_sentence: T'essafaġ ḥit un ṯeqqim bu lmakla ɛaḏ.
example.source_sentence: I’m sorry that you have no food left.
example.description: ‘No … left’ is expressed by negating ‘remain’ (**u(n) teqqim**) plus **ɛaḏ** ‘anymore/no longer’.
Documents note **walu** as ‘nothing,’ often used for existential/localization denials without the usual postverbal negator.

sections.description: For ‘there is/are not,’ Senhaja zrqet uses the existential **makayen** (‘there isn’t’). The negative quantifier **walu** ‘nothing’ can carry existential or locative negation by itself. To convey ‘no X left’ or ‘X no longer exists,’ speakers often negate a verb of persistence like ‘remain/stay’ together with the aspectual adverb **ɛaḏ** ‘anymore/no longer.’

sections.focus: Negative indefinites and polarity items: walu, ḥed(d), ši, ḥetta ši

example.target_sentence: zazekka nnes qqimeġ tražayeġ. uḏḏyusi ḥed.
example.description: Constituent negation with **ḥed** ‘nobody’; the verbal frame **uḏ …** is used with the coming event.

example.target_sentence: ṯenna-s < la walu > ṯenna-s < u-ṯernum ḥetta ḏ lmerra ḏ yewweṯ u-ṯernum-š >
example.source_sentence: She told her, "Don't add even one more time, don't add it."
example.description: Answer particle **la** ‘no’ plus **walu** ‘nothing’; **ḥetta** ‘even’ strengthens the prohibition alongside **u … -š**.

example.target_sentence: ṯenna-s < kif-aš? u ṯarbaṯ u-s-ṯeḡḡi walu. ṯarbaṯ-iya yella ṯeffġ-id ag-uḫyam, u-s-ṯeḡḡi ḥetta šihaža .>
example.source_sentence: "This girl she just came out by the house, she did not do to him even a little."
example.description: Constituent negation with **walu** ‘nothing’; **ḥetta** + **šihaža** ‘even a little’ strengthens the denial.
Only the matrix verb ‘think’ is negated with **u … -š**; the complement uses **ši ḥaža** ‘anything,’ showing scope on the matrix predicate.

example.target_sentence: u nekk, ṯamġarṯ aqqa weṣṣiḫt uṯfettaḥ ṯawwerṯ gi ḥtta ši niwwen .
example.source_sentence: and I have told my wife, to not open the door to anyone,
example.description: Negative imperative context with **ḥtta ši niwwen** ‘even one/anyone’ as a negative polarity item under the negation.
Documents show **walu** ‘nothing’ replacing the usual postverbal **-š**, marking constituent negation of the object.

sections.description: Senhaja zrqet uses negative indefinites and polarity items to focus the negation on a constituent: **walu** ‘nothing,’ **ḥed(d)** ‘nobody,’ **ši** ‘some/any’ (which means ‘any’ under negation), and **ḥetta ši** ‘even one/any at all’ to strengthen the denial. With such items, the usual postverbal **-š** may be absent, because the negative meaning is already carried by the quantifier.

example.target_sentence: Simana nna yeɛḏan, arḇa ynu iwġulid z gmalu kši lfakiha ġriḇa ɛemmer ẓẓṛiḵt.
example.source_sentence: Last week, my child came back from the forest with some strange fruit I had never seen.
example.description: ‘Never’ is expressed with **ɛemmer** preceding a formally positive verb; the **u … -š** frame is typically not used here.

example.target_sentence: La, tlaqiġ ši lɛa'ila neḵ, walakin ɛemmer ẓriġ tṣawer neḵ.
example.source_sentence: Well, I’ve met some of your relatives, but I’ve never seen your pictures.
example.description: Again **ɛemmer** ‘never’ licenses negation without the circumfix on the verb.

example.target_sentence: walakin maši ḏ arḇa ɛaḏ, mmġuraġ daḇa.
example.source_sentence: But I’m not a child any more, I’ve grown up.
example.description: **ɛaḏ** expresses ‘no longer/any more’ together with nominal negation **maši ḏ**.

example.target_sentence: U lmeddraṣa qḏima dba gaɛ u teqqim.
example.source_sentence: That was an old school that doesn’t exist anymore.
example.description: ‘No longer exists’ conveyed by a negated ‘remain/stay’ verb plus an ‘anymore’ setting.

example.target_sentence: T'essafaġ ḥit un ṯeqqim bu lmakla ɛaḏ.
example.source_sentence: I’m sorry that you have no food left.
example.description: ‘No … left’ = negated ‘remain’ (**u(n) teqqim**) + **ɛaḏ** ‘anymore/no longer’.
Documents illustrate ‘never’ combining a negative temporal adverb with future/irrealis marking and the postverbal **-š**.

sections.description: Temporal meaning matters. For ‘never,’ Senhaja zrqet uses **ɛemmer** placed before the verb; in such cases, the verb often stays formally positive (no **-š**). For ‘no longer/anymore,’ **ɛaḏ** is used, often together with a negated predicate or with a verb like ‘remain’ to say that nothing is left. The documents also show ‘never’ in a future context built with additional morphology.

sections.focus: Scope: matrix vs. embedded negation; possession vs. relation

example.description: The negation **u … -š** applies only to the matrix verb ‘think’ (scope on the thinking), while the embedded part uses **ši ḥaža** ‘anything’ without its own negation.

example.target_sentence: waš maši ḏ ddefṭar n ttamarin nnem?
example.description: Identification is denied with **maši ḏ + NP**. The scope is on the relation ‘be (yours),’ not on the existence of the notebook.
Documents show possessive/existential negation with a negated ‘be’ structure; scope on existence/association (‘there is not [him] with me’).
Same meaning expressed with **maši**; again scope is on the association rather than on identifying a specific entity.

example.target_sentence: u nekk, ṯamġarṯ aqqa weṣṣiḫt uṯfettaḥ ṯawwerṯ gi ḥtta ši niwwen .
example.source_sentence: and I have told my wife, to not open the door to anyone,
example.description: Here the negation targets the permission/action; **ḥtta ši niwwen** ‘even one/anyone’ shows scope over the potential door-opener (constituent negation).

sections.description: Scope can be narrow (only the main verb is denied) or broad (the whole proposition). With **u … -š** on a matrix verb like ‘think,’ the complement can contain **ši** ‘any’ instead of being separately negated, which shows narrow scope. For possession/association, Senhaja zrqet can negate existence/association (‘there is not [X] with me’) or negate the relational identification (‘this isn’t mine’) using **maši ḏ**.

sections.focus: Negative questions, answers, and form variation

example.target_sentence: waš maši ḏ ddefṭar n ttamarin nnem?
example.description: Negative question built with the interrogative **waš** plus **maši ḏ** for nominal negation.
**La** is used as the standalone answer ‘no’; clausal negation still uses **u … -š**.
Answer **la** ‘no’ followed by existential **makayen** and **ḥetta ši** ‘any at all.’
Shows the postverbal variant **-ši** and a **ma-** element with an adjectival predicate.

example.source_sentence: he arose that man, he does not see well.
example.description: Illustrates a preverbal variant **uḏ**. The documents note other variants like **ur**, and postverbal **-š/-ši/-šay** depending on context/dialect.

sections.description: Negative questions can combine the question particle **waš** with nominal negation **maši ḏ**. The particle **la** serves as a standalone ‘no.’ Forms vary across contexts: preverbal **u/ur/uḏ**, postverbal **-š/-ši/-šay**, and sometimes aspect-specific items like **ma** (Aorist) or **la/ula** (Imperfective) are mentioned in the documents. Both sources agree on the bipartite nature of verbal negation; they differ mainly in terminology (the sentence analysis calls it a verbal circumfix; the documents call them particles).

conclusion: What to remember: The default way to negate a verb in Senhaja zrqet is the two-part frame **u … -š** around the finite verb. For nouns and identification, use **maši ḏ + NP** or a negated ‘be’-type structure. For existence, use **makayen** or **walu**; to say ‘no longer/none left,’ negate a ‘remain’ verb with **ɛaḏ**. Negative indefinites like **walu**, **ḥed**, and **ḥetta ši** handle constituent negation and often make the postverbal **-š** unnecessary. ‘Never’ is **ɛemmer** before the verb. Orthography and form vary (**u/ur/uḏ**, **-š/-ši**), and **la** is the answer ‘no.’
